repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-AMG GT 63 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Restores refinement — worth it on a £100k+ car
Replacement often £7,500–£12,000 — assess vehicle value carefully

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes-AMG GT 63? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented timing chain tensioner history (especially on higher-mileage examples)
* Full Mercedes-AMG service history with regular oil changes (8,000–10,000 miles)
* Turbo oil feed line recall work completed
avoidIf: * Any evidence of cold-start rattle — timing chain tensioner wear is serious
* Oil smell or smoke from engine bay — turbo oil line leak risk
* Poor service history — the M177 is unforgiving of neglect
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The AMG GT 63 is a genuine performance icon — a 585PS twin-turbo V8 in a stunning four-door body. The M177 'hot-vee' V8 is fundamentally robust but demands respect for its heat management and timing chain tensioners. Buy on history, maintain the oil and cooling systems religiously, and the GT 63 will reward you.

cta.label: → Compare Mercedes-AMG GT 63 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-AMG GT 63 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup (timing chain tensioner wear warning)
* Service History — 8,000–10,000 mile oil changes are essential; check for Mercedes-AMG spec
* Turbo Oil Line Recall — Has the recall been completed? Check with Mercedes dealer
* Oil Temperature — Monitor during test drive; any unusually high readings?
* Coolant Level — Check for any loss — hot-vee cooling system is critical
* Engine Vibration — Any shaking at idle? Engine mount wear is common
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Resolve before buying
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-AMG GT 63 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned Mercedes-AMG GT 63 engine (M177.980) typically costs £7,500–£12,000 supply only. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500. These are specialist-only replacements — the hot-vee V8 requires Mercedes-AMG expertise. A timing chain tensioner replacement costs £2,000–£4,500.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-AMG GT 63 engine reliable?
items.answer: The M177 4.0-litre V8 is fundamentally robust and is the same core engine used across AMG's 63 models. However, the 'hot-vee' design puts turbos inside the V of the engine, creating significant heat — oil and cooling system maintenance are critical. Timing chain tensioners can wear. Early examples had turbo oil feed line recalls. With proper maintenance, these engines are very reliable.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-AMG GT 63?
items.answer: Timing chain tensioner wear (cold-start rattle, £2,000–£4,500), hot-vee cooling system issues (coolant loss, overheating, £500–£1,500), turbo oil feed line leaks on early models (£800–£2,000), and engine mount wear (shaking at idle, £600–£1,500).

items.question: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-AMG GT 63 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: GT 63 values range from £50,000–£120,000 depending on year, condition and mileage. A £7,500–£12,000 reconditioned engine represents 10–20% of vehicle value — well within our 70% Mercedes-Benz scrap threshold. For most examples, replacement is financially viable.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-AMG GT 63 engine code?
items.answer: The GT 63 uses the M177.980 engine code — Mercedes-AMG's 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 producing 585PS. This is the same engine family used across AMG's 63 models. The code is stamped on the engine block; your VIN and vehicle documentation will also confirm the specification.

items.question: Can I repair the timing chain tensioner issue, or does it need replacement?
items.answer: Yes — if caught early. Timing chain and tensioner replacement costs £2,000–£4,500 and is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement (£7,500–£12,000). A worn tensioner will give the timing chain too much slack, leading to a slapping noise, especially on startup. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately — ignored chain wear can lead to catastrophic engine failure.
